summary: The State of DevOps Report is an annual survey-based study by Nicole Forsgren, Jez Humble, and Gene Kim that uses statistical analysis to identify effective software delivery practices. The report surveys tens of thousands of developers using carefully designed questions that measure constructs indirectly rather than asking direct questions that would yield unreliable answers. A key finding is that organizations cluster into four performance tiers (elite, high, medium, low), with elite performers deploying multiple times daily while low performers take months. Crucially, high throughput does not sacrifice stability—elite teams have lower failure rates and faster recovery times than their slower counterparts.
key_points: Survey questions are designed to measure constructs indirectly because direct questions like 'do you do CI' give unreliable answers
Organizations cluster into four distinct performance tiers based on deployment frequency, lead time, time to restore, and change failure rate
Elite performers deploy many times daily with under one hour lead time, while low performers take months
High throughput correlates with better stability—elite teams have 0-15% failure rates versus 46-60% for low performers
The four key metrics are deployment frequency, lead time, time to restore, and change failure rate
money_quotes: Elite performers deploy many times a day, taking under an hour to take a change completed by a developer into production.
Low performers, in contrast, take months to deploy a change into production.

This high throughput does not come at the cost of system stability, since elite teams have a change failure rate of less than 15% (compared to 46-60% for low performance teams) and can recover from a failure in minutes rather than weeks.
They don't ask 'do you do continuous integration' (which we know would give very unreliable answers), but instead ask for concrete things that are part of CI.
key_insight: Elite software delivery teams achieve both speed and stability—deploying many times daily with lower failure rates than teams that deploy monthly, proving that velocity and quality are complementary rather than competing goals.
